item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the test log is fun and I did enjoy reading this, so I upvoted. That said, I do believe this article could benefit from fixing some of the issues addressed by psul.

Also, as a side note, the bird's feelings of remorse don't come across to me as genuine. Actually, it seems more like the bird was pretending to feel guilty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is just bad, I would say in which ways exactly but I see psul did that 2 years ago. So I'll just note down the outstanding issues:

1. Poor tone all throught the piece. It mars it down and makes it seem amateurish and uninteresting.
2. The base idea is okay-ish but the execution isn't done well at all
3. You have to go to the test log to know exactly what the skip does and that's a major no-no
4. All-powerful items with shallow and uninteresting personalities are boring. You can do better

Nothing grabs me as I read this, I read it until the end just because that's what I do, not because I was enjoying myself. I suggest a full rewrite. Until then, have a downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree completely on all points. Aside from fixing the unprofessional tone and the general writing errors, I think this SCP could use a full rewrite. I see two potential options for a rewrite:

Option 1

The story stays mostly the same, and we just make everything more clinical. The discovery comes as a separate section, after a coherent description of the anomaly. It sounds like a child-like entity with reality-bending powers has been bound to a "dipping bird" toy. This is inherently dangerous, regardless of the intent of the entity, which was made clear when it killed an entire family with fire.

The discovery itself needs to be fleshed out more. The fire department should definitely have discovered the bodies of the family, though the cause of the fire remains unknown. The SCP will be found and noted as odd because it survived the fire intact, despite being made of no special heat-resistant materials. Or, in keeping with the theme that the entity feels remorse, it could be found in a small bubble of no damage, as it tried (unsuccessfully) to take back what it did. Though, this presents some holes, as if it is a reality-bender, it should have been just as easy to put the fire out as it was to start it.

The experiment log needs to be seriously cut down. Keeping in mind that it is a reality-bender, it would be much more interesting if it warped its containment to fit its pre-fire surroundings. It could even warp D-class into imitations of "Johnny."

Lastly, the final addendum can stay, but it is far to emotional for Foundation standards. Also, it contradicts itself. The first paragraph seems to state that the reason testing is to be stopped is because "some emotional distress that we have appeared to have caused." However, the second paragraph states "we have better things to work on than its feelings." Testing could be stopped out of concern for the entity's well-being, but that recommendation would be better served coming from the Ethics Committee, rather than the project director.

Option 2

We make the SCP a lot darker, and bump it's classification up to Euclid. The basic story stays the same, and the changes in Option 1 still need to be made, but the story and tone will change. It will be a reality bender that saw the person it cared about being abused, and its response was to burn the parents alive, and kill the kid in his sleep. The major change here will be that SCP-2671 does not feel remorse. However, over the course of testing, and the questions asked to it by researchers, it begins to doubt its actions. This one could even end in Neutralization, if it chooses to self-immolate, rather than face the guilt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ^ even if I like this, this is some really good critique.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has all sorts of issues leading me to downvote:

  • lots of SPAG errors (eg "in the even"), clumsy phrasing and formatting and weak clinical tone
  • giving the recovery information before describing the anomaly.
  • the recovery story not being believable - fire investigators (or the Foundation taking over from them) would be able to determine that there were 3 human corpses in that burned-out building. As such "the location of the family is still unknown" makes little sense.
  • the conditions for the anomalous effect being unclear (eg does "entered a room along with SCP-2671" mean that they must bring it with them, or that SCP-2671 must already be in the room?)
  • a "can make anything happen" anomaly, with predictably dull results, and an unnecessarily long testing log (no mystery to solve, no particular escalation, no surprises, some rather trite character development that takes too many iterations).
  • an utterly pat backstory/motivation, which results in the Foundation seeming incredibly thick - they still think "how the fire was started … is still unknown" despite the hints from the anomaly?
  • needless notes in the final addendum that don't take the story forward.

This is one of the weakest upvoted articles that I've read recently.

item.title: Re: STAFF POST-OPEN
item.content:encoded: Hey just wondering, could you mention to some of the higher-up's if you could add something to the image use page for those who use their OWN pictures. It really isn't that clear and goes more over on what to do if your're borrowing a picture.

item.title: Re: STAFF POST-OPEN
item.content:encoded: K, thx

item.title: Re: STAFF POST-OPEN
item.content:encoded:
You may want to put some statement in there about how you're releasing them under CC-BY-SA 3.0.

I mean, you already have by posting them, but a formal statement is a nice feelgood.

item.title: Re: STAFF POST-OPEN
item.content:encoded:
Both images were made by me, the author of SCP-2671.

Original Source: ZapperTex
Creator: ZapperTex

http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-2671

This is also released under the CC-BY-SA 3.0. Link has been provided below
https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

yes pastarasta1 you were right. It doesn't say on the image policy page what to do with your OWN work.

item.title: Re: STAFF POST-OPEN
item.content:encoded:
I honestly feel like both images where created by the author himself, I am doing an image search on google tho.

EDIT : I used image search, and the only pictures that popped up where the one on here and the one on the sandbox, so I bet it was made by the author.

item.title: STAFF POST-OPEN
item.content:encoded: Issue solved. Thanks for your help!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love the quirkiness, like I mentioned with your draft (: No vote for now because I feel some areas are a bit clunky, but it's certainly not bad, in the slightest. The story, to me, is warranted and supports the narrative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Had a good chuckle from pity. I love sad stuff that's just enough to make you go "oh no!"

That said, I also agree that the test log needs some tidying up. I found it rather difficult to go through.

I have a problem with this part:

Its transitions are virtually instantaneous, even with examinations of light-speed camera footage capable of capturing 1 trillion FPS.

I feel like this is a detail that really doesn't need to be included. The ability to instantaneously manipulate space should warrant its own surprise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually rather like this. A little too on the nose with its backstory, but it was interesting to see an inconsistent anomaly go through the motions. Also reminded me of Oyasumi Punpun, being a surreal and dark story about a bird thing.

The formatting of the test logs were kinda messy, though. I would put those in quote boxes to improve readability.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dave McCree, keeping the Foundation cold.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoops, forgot the space after the carrot, thanks man

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Test 2671-19 : Procedure in test 2671-17 repeated.

Test Results
SCP-2671 held the miniature white board and had written the following in small text."

Nothing appears after it, did you forget to add what he wrote?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty much the reason I downvoted. I got through half of it without having a solid idea of what's supposed to be going on, but not in an interesting/mysterious way like in, for example, Tabula Rasa. It failed to capture my interest, which is a death sentence. Slow burns can work, but here it feels like the pacing is wonky.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find the description to be somewhat problematic. You have taken a lot of time describing the satellite points (like how it was recovered), and it took me up to the test log to have some idea about the anomaly.

I think that the anomaly should be the first thing worth mentioning, since this is supposed to be a Foundation article and Foundation articles focus on the anomaly. That can allow better comprehension for the reader, and allow the logs to be enjoyed more IMO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Poor Dippy Bu(r)ddy. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ding ding ding, correct

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So as far as I decypher the drawing I see a happy kid owning the dippy-bird toy. Then abusive parents making the kid sad and the toy angry. Angry toy comes to the aid of the kid with its anomalous powers and accidentally burns down the entire place, kid included.

And then follows sadness for what it has done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The crayon drawing is nice, but the abusive parents angle has been done to death, and the tone feels really off for a reason I can't put my finger on.

On second thought, novote since I haven't yet read the entire thing.

Yeah I shouldn't judge a book by its cover.

Headcanon : The "abuse" was really just spanking, since it gives an even darker spin on this.
